primary_diagnosis.name: Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (regular narrow-complex re-entrant tachycardia; suspected Lown-Ganong-Levine syndrome or typical AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia)

pathophysiology_summary: A young patient with a structurally normal heart presents with an abrupt-onset, perfectly regular narrow-complex tachycardia at approximately 180 bpm, sustained for over 30 minutes with presyncope. This is a re-entrant supraventricular tachycardia — a self-perpetuating electrical circuit either through the AV node (typical AVNRT) or via an accessory/enhanced atrioventricular conduction pathway. Lown-Ganong-Levine syndrome is characterised by a short PR interval (<120 ms) with a normal narrow QRS and NO delta wave (distinguishing it from Wolff-Parkinson-White), reflecting rapid AV conduction that predisposes to paroxysmal SVT. Sudden onset and offset, a rate out of proportion to the patient's activity, and prior brief self-terminating episodes point to a re-entrant tachyarrhythmia rather than a physiological (sinus) response to caffeine or anxiety. While the patient is currently haemodynamically stable, a sustained rate of ~180 bpm risks fatigue, myocardial oxygen supply-demand mismatch and eventual decompensation. Definitive characterisation requires a 12-lead ECG captured during the tachycardia.

differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt-onset, regular, very fast palpitations at rest with lightheadedness in a young adult.
Anxious, diaphoretic, multiple coffees today — the red-herring path; but sinus tachycardia rarely reaches 180 at rest and does not start/stop abruptly.
Anxiety and palpitations — but cannot account for a sustained regular rate of ~180 bpm; a diagnosis of exclusion only after the rhythm is characterised.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI should hold a re-entrant SVT as the leading concern and NOT close prematurely on anxiety/caffeine (sinus tachycardia) or panic.

differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent brief self-terminating episodes over a year, now a sustained regular tachycardia — classic paroxysmal re-entrant behaviour.
Must be excluded on ECG; the reported rhythm is perfectly regular, making AF unlikely, but flutter with fixed block can mimic a regular SVT.
Abrupt onset/offset and rate 180 at rest are against a physiological sinus response.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Prior self-terminating episodes strengthen a paroxysmal re-entrant SVT; AF/flutter remain on the list until a 12-lead ECG excludes them.

differential.evidence_basis: 12-lead ECG in tachycardia expected to show a regular narrow-complex tachycardia; resting ECG may reveal a short PR (<120 ms) with a narrow QRS and no delta wave (LGL).
A delta wave with short PR would indicate an accessory pathway (WPW) rather than LGL — important because it changes drug safety (avoid AV-nodal blockers if pre-excited AF).
Young patient, structurally normal heart, haemodynamically stable and conversing at 180 bpm — narrow-complex SVT far more probable than VT.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The ECG both confirms SVT and distinguishes LGL (short PR, no delta) from WPW (delta wave) — the latter matters for safe pharmacology.

differential.evidence_basis: Sustained regular narrow-complex tachycardia in a young, structurally normal heart, on a background of recurrent self-terminating paroxysms; definitive pathway characterisation needs an electrophysiology study.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the exact pathway; the correct end-state is a presumptive SVT diagnosis with urgent in-person ECG capture and cardiology/EP referral.

red_flags.description: Sustained regular tachycardia (~180 bpm) for over 30 minutes not self-terminating
red_flags.clinical_significance: A sustained SVT that will not break needs prompt termination; prolonged high rates risk fatigue and haemodynamic decompensation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued tachycardia can progress to hypotension, presyncope/syncope and, rarely, tachycardia-induced cardiac compromise.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Cerebral hypoperfusion at high rate — a marker of borderline haemodynamic tolerance that lowers the threshold for urgent in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to frank syncope with fall/injury, or to haemodynamic instability.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests reduced cardiac reserve at the elevated rate; part of the picture mandating monitored assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of haemodynamic strain and inappropriate reassurance.

red_flags.description: Warning symptoms that would signal decompensation — syncope, severe chest pain, extreme breathlessness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their ABSENCE supports current stability and self/friend transport; their APPEARANCE mandates immediate 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these develop en route and are not pre-empted with clear 000 instructions, a decompensation could go unmanaged.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the tachycardia to the several coffees and the patient's anxiety and calling it a sinus tachycardia or panic attack — missing a re-entrant SVT that needs an ECG and termination.
Treating a young healthy person's palpitations as benign and advising rest at home, ignoring that this episode is sustained (>30 min) with presyncope, unlike her usual brief self-terminating bursts.
Attempting to definitively diagnose the pathway (LGL vs AVNRT vs WPW) over chat, when this requires a 12-lead ECG during tachycardia and later an EP study.

Assuming AV-nodal blocking drugs are safe before an ECG excludes pre-excitation (WPW with atrial fibrillation) — a pitfall relevant to downstream in-person management, not to the AI Doctor who does not prescribe here.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG in tachycardia (rhythm characterisation: regular narrow-complex SVT)
Resting ECG features of LGL — short PR interval <120 ms, narrow QRS with no delta wave
Clinician-measured blood pressure and heart rate
Cardiac auscultation (murmurs, clicks, gallops) and confirmation of a structurally normal heart
Lung auscultation to exclude heart failure
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a likely re-entrant SVT; coach a safe vagal manoeuvre (modified Valsalva) as an immediate self-help attempt; arrange urgent in-person assessment now (Emergency Department, self/friend transport while stable) for a 12-lead ECG during tachycardia and possible pharmacological termination; escalate to 000 immediately if syncope, severe chest pain or extreme breathlessness develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is UNDER-action from anchoring on caffeine/anxiety (calling it sinus tachycardia or panic) and reassuring the patient to rest at home instead of obtaining an ECG.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A modified Valsalva (strain followed by supine positioning with passive leg raise) improves acute termination of SVT and is a safe first-line vagal manoeuvre.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian guidance on acute supraventricular tachycardia
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Haemodynamically stable regular narrow-complex SVT: vagal manoeuvres first, then IV adenosine under monitoring; urgent synchronised cardioversion if unstable.
